sections.heading: Background and the Apostles' Creed
sections.html:
The Council of Trent and the Catechism's Purpose

The Roman Catechism — formally the Catechismus ex decreto Concilii Tridentini — was commissioned by the Council of Trent and published under Pope Pius V in 1566. Its purpose was fundamentally pastoral: to equip parish priests with a sound, comprehensive summary of Catholic doctrine in the aftermath of the Protestant Reformation. Unlike the small personal catechisms of Luther or Canisius, the Roman Catechism was written for priests, not children — it presupposes a preacher who will draw on it for homilies and instruction.

The catechism is organized around four pillars of Christian formation: the Apostles' Creed (what we believe), the Sacraments (how grace is conferred), the Ten Commandments (how we are to live), and the Lord's Prayer (how we are to pray). This fourfold structure reflects the Council's conviction that faith, worship, morality, and prayer are inseparable dimensions of Christian life. The structure was later adopted by the Catechism of the Catholic Church (1992), which follows the same four-part architecture.

The Council of Trent (1545–1563) met in three periods to address doctrinal errors, define disputed points, and reform ecclesiastical abuses. Its decrees on Scripture and Tradition, original sin, justification, and the sacraments provide the dogmatic foundation on which the Roman Catechism rests. The catechism is not itself a conciliar decree but a magisterial teaching document of the highest pastoral authority.

The Apostles' Creed: God, Trinity, and Creation

The Roman Catechism devotes its first and most extensive section to the twelve articles of the Apostles' Creed, treating each article with doctrinal precision and homiletical warmth. The first article — "I believe in God the Father almighty, creator of heaven and earth" — grounds everything in the existence, unity, and creative power of God.

The catechism insists that the word "Father" applies to God in three senses: he is Father of the eternal Son by an ineffable generation; he is Father of all rational creatures by creation; and he is Father of the justified by adoption and grace. These are not three separate relationships but one divine paternity expressed in different modes. The scriptural basis is Paul's great doxology: "For from him and through him and to him are all things. To him be glory forever" (Rom 11:36).

On the Trinity, the catechism expounds the unity of substance and distinction of persons with care. The Father is unbegotten; the Son is eternally begotten of the Father (John 1:14; Ps 2:7); the Holy Spirit proceeds from both Father and Son (the Filioque). Each person is distinct but none is greater or lesser: "The Father is God, the Son is God, and the Holy Spirit is God; yet there are not three Gods but one God." Creation, including the creation of angels and the ordering of time, is attributed primarily to the Father as the principle without principle within the Trinity.

  • Gen 1:1 — "In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth."
  • John 1:3 — All things were made through the Word.
  • Col 1:16 — All things created in, through, and for Christ.

The Creed: Incarnation, Passion, Resurrection, and Judgment

The catechism's treatment of the Christological articles is particularly rich. The Incarnation is presented as the supreme act of divine condescension: the eternal Son, co-equal with the Father, emptied himself, taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of men (Phil 2:6–8). The catechism carefully distinguishes the virginal conception from all natural generation, affirming that the Holy Spirit overshadowed Mary (Luke 1:35) so that the one born of her is truly God.

The Passion narratives receive extended treatment. Christ's suffering was voluntary — "I lay down my life that I may take it up again" (John 10:17) — and redemptive, constituting a sacrifice of infinite worth that more than suffices for the sins of all humanity. The catechism teaches that Christ descended to the realm of the dead not to suffer further punishment but to announce his victory and release the souls of the just who had waited in hope for the Redeemer.

The Resurrection is treated as bodily and historical, evidenced by the apostolic witness and the empty tomb. Christ's risen body is the same body that was crucified, now glorified and impassible. His Ascension to the right hand of the Father signifies his enthronement as universal Lord (Eph 1:20–21). The final article of the Creed — the life everlasting — is the eschatological horizon toward which all Christian existence is oriented, encompassing the resurrection of the body, the general judgment, and the eternal communion of the saints in the beatific vision.

sections.heading: The Sacraments
sections.html:
The Sacraments Defined and Distinguished

The Roman Catechism provides one of the most thorough pre-modern expositions of sacramental theology. A sacrament is defined as a visible sign of an invisible grace, instituted by Christ for our sanctification. The catechism distinguishes Catholic sacramental doctrine from Protestant views on three key points: first, the sacraments truly confer grace (ex opere operato) and are not merely commemorative rites or pledges of faith; second, all seven sacraments were instituted by Christ, not merely two; and third, the minister's personal holiness does not affect the validity of the sacrament, which depends on proper matter, form, and intention.

The seven sacraments are enumerated and given a theological order: three sacraments of Christian initiation (Baptism, Confirmation, Eucharist), two sacraments of healing (Penance, Anointing of the Sick), and two sacraments of vocation and mission (Holy Orders, Matrimony). This ordering reflects the shape of the Christian life from birth to death, and from individual sanctification to ecclesial mission.

  • John 3:5 — Baptism as rebirth of water and Spirit.
  • Acts 8:14–17 — The laying on of hands in Confirmation.
  • 1 Cor 11:23–26 — The Eucharist as memorial and proclamation of the Lord's death.

Baptism, Confirmation, Eucharist, and Transubstantiation

Baptism is the door of the sacraments and of the Church. It removes original sin and all personal sins, infuses sanctifying grace, the theological virtues, and the gifts of the Holy Spirit, and impresses an indelible spiritual character. The catechism addresses the baptism of infants, defending the practice as consonant with the doctrine of original sin: since children inherit Adam's sin, they need the remedy of Baptism regardless of personal guilt.

The Eucharist receives the most extended treatment of all the sacraments. The catechism defends the doctrine of transubstantiation — the conversion of the entire substance of the bread and wine into the substance of the body and blood of Christ, while the accidents (appearance, taste, texture) of bread and wine remain. This teaching, defined at the Fourth Lateran Council (1215) and reaffirmed at Trent, is grounded in Christ's words at the Last Supper: "This is my body... this is my blood" (Matt 26:26–28), and in the Bread of Life discourse: "For my flesh is true food, and my blood is true drink" (John 6:55).

The Mass is presented not merely as a commemoration but as a true sacrifice — the same sacrifice as Calvary offered in an unbloody manner. This is not a repetition of Calvary but its perpetual re-presentation before the Father. The Letter to the Hebrews' insistence on the one sacrifice of Christ (Heb 10:14) is harmonized with the perpetual offering of Malachi's prophecy: "In every place incense will be offered to my name, and a pure offering" (Mal 1:11).

Penance and Holy Orders

Penance is the sacrament by which sins committed after Baptism are forgiven through the absolution of a priest. The catechism identifies three acts of the penitent as integral to the sacrament: contrition (sorrow for sin because it offends God), confession (the oral accusation of sins to a priest), and satisfaction (performing the penance assigned by the confessor). Perfect contrition, arising from love of God, forgives sins even before sacramental absolution; imperfect contrition (attrition) arising from fear of punishment suffices for the sacrament.

The institution of Penance is traced to the Risen Christ's words to the apostles: "Receive the Holy Spirit. If you forgive the sins of any, they are forgiven them; if you withhold forgiveness from any, it is withheld" (John 20:22–23). The binding and loosing authority granted to Peter (Matt 16:19) and to all the apostles (Matt 18:18) is the authority transmitted in Holy Orders to exercise judicial and ministerial functions in the Church.

Holy Orders imprints an indelible character and configures the ordained to Christ as head, shepherd, and priest. The catechism distinguishes the episcopate, presbyterate, and diaconate as the three degrees of orders. The authority of the priest to celebrate the Eucharist and absolve sins is not self-derived but participates in the one priesthood of Christ — "You are a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek" (Heb 5:6) — exercised through the ministerial priesthood for the benefit of the whole Body.

sections.heading: The Commandments and Prayer
sections.html:
The Ten Commandments

The Roman Catechism uses the Catholic (and Lutheran) numbering of the Ten Commandments, which differs from the Reformed numbering at the first and last commandments. In the Catholic enumeration, the prohibition of graven images is treated as part of the first commandment (honoring no other gods) rather than a separate second commandment, and the tenth commandment is divided into two (coveting one's neighbor's wife; coveting one's neighbor's goods).

The catechism reads the commandments in the light of the New Law. Christ's declaration — "Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them" (Matt 5:17) — means that the moral precepts of the Decalogue remain binding in the new covenant, though their deeper meaning is now revealed. Thus the prohibition of murder extends to anger and contempt (Matt 5:21–22); the prohibition of adultery extends to lustful looking (Matt 5:27–28).

The catechism distinguishes between precepts (obligations binding all Christians) and counsels (voluntary commitments such as chastity, poverty, and obedience that mark the religious life). Both are paths of love, but the counsels represent a more total self-offering to God. Paul's treatment of celibacy in 1 Cor 7:32–34 is the foundational text for the theology of the evangelical counsels.

  • Ex 20:1–17 — The Decalogue at Sinai.
  • Deut 5:6–21 — Moses' repetition of the commandments.
  • Rom 13:9 — The commandments summed up in the love of neighbor.

The Lord's Prayer

The fourth and final part of the Roman Catechism is devoted to prayer, and specifically to the Lord's Prayer — the model and measure of all Christian prayer. The catechism follows the Matthean version (Matt 6:9–13) with its seven petitions, and provides a pastoral exposition of each.

"Our Father who art in heaven" — the address establishes the speaker's relationship to God: not a distant monarch but an intimate Father who has adopted us through grace. The plural "our" indicates that Christian prayer is never merely private but always ecclesial. "Hallowed be thy name" — we pray that God's name be treated with reverence by all people, beginning with ourselves. "Thy kingdom come" — we pray for the increase of the Church, the conversion of sinners, and the final consummation of all things in Christ. "Thy will be done on earth as it is in heaven" — we pray that our wills be conformed to God's, as the saints and angels in heaven perfectly obey. "Give us this day our daily bread" — the catechism notes a double meaning: temporal sustenance for the body and the Eucharistic bread of eternal life. "Forgive us our trespasses as we forgive those who trespass against us" — this petition carries its own condition; the one who refuses to forgive cannot expect forgiveness (Matt 18:35). "Lead us not into temptation but deliver us from evil" — we do not pray to be exempt from trial but to be strengthened in it, after the example of Christ's own agony in Gethsemane (Matt 26:39).

The Theological Virtues and the Last Things

The Roman Catechism places the theological virtues — faith, hope, and charity — at the center of the Christian moral and spiritual life. These are called theological because their object is God himself, and they cannot be acquired by human effort but are infused by grace at Baptism. Paul's great hymn to charity in 1 Cor 13:13 — "So now faith, hope, and love abide, these three; but the greatest of these is love" — establishes the hierarchy: charity is the form of all virtues, the bond of perfection (Col 3:14).

The catechism concludes with the Last Things as it began with the first things, framing the whole of Christian existence between creation and consummation. Death remains the universal human condition: "It is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment" (Heb 9:27). The particular judgment determines the immediate destination of the soul; the general judgment at the end of time is the public vindication of divine justice before all creation. Heaven is the direct vision of God — the lumen gloriae — which constitutes perfect blessedness. Hell is the eternal state of those who die in final impenitence, separated from God whose love they have permanently refused. Purgatory is the Church's confident teaching on the merciful purification of those who die in grace but still burdened with the temporal effects of forgiven sin.

  • 1 John 3:2 — "We shall see him as he is" — the beatific vision foretold.
  • Matt 25:46 — Eternal punishment and eternal life set in contrast.
  • Rev 21:3–4 — God dwelling with his people; no more death or mourning.
